speaker: interviewee
text: Af-- after the liberation, when we were liberated by the British army--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was April 15, 1944-- 1945, 1945, right. They were very helpful. They set up a hospital. And they gave us very good care.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see them come in? What was it like when they first came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we were very happy. Everybody was cheering. And everybody was very happy to-- to live to see the end of the war and to be liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew who they were, yes. And they brought us water, which was the most necessary, the first thing that we wanted. And then they took us to a place where they washed us and they bathed us. And they washed our hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they even complimented. How come? And we don't have to cut your hair. It's not so bad as some of theirs. It's not so infected with lice. How come? I said because my sister cleaned my hair, and I cleaned hers. And we tried to do as much for ourselves as we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to a hospital. And I had typhus. And I was very sick. I had high fever. I couldn't digest any food. And I stood there for two months in the hospital till I got to myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in a different hospital. She went first because they took the ones who are-- she was in worse condition than I was. And then I went. And then we were together again after the war, after the liberation, after the hospital. We lived together. And we were planning to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you got out of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Bergen-Belsen. And we were planning to go back to Romania to see-- we were hoping that some of our family members survived. And there was a group going to Satu Mare. And we took what was most necessary, what we had too-- whatever we could take us, food, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody came back from my hometown and told us not to go home. Nobody survived from my family. And my sister, who was married before the war-- and you know what happened to her husband-- was hoping to see her husband. And probably, she thought that he went home waiting for her. But when we heard that nobody went home, we had lost courage to go home. And we-- I cried. And I said, I don't want to go. If there's nobody left, there's nothing there for me, and I'm not going. So then, not long after, my sister heard from her husband that he survived, and he's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd she hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had parents in the States from before the war. And they sent out lists from the camps to the States. And they put it up in places like social places, where people-- shuls and organizations that these are the people who survived. And her mother-in-law, father-in-law saw her name on the list. They notified the husband. They wrote to him that your wife is alive. And she's there. She's in Bergen-Belsen. He arranged to send her legal papers. He was allowed to. As a husband, the wife could send legal because those days was under the British occupation. They were not allowed to go-- Jewish people they didn't want to receive. He sent her a certificate that she should emigrate. She went to France by herself, to Marseilles. And from there, she sailed. She went to Israel. And I was left alone. But I had very nice friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions were not bad. We had decent housing, food. We had to go-- there was a social kitchen where they had foods the Joint provided. And they were sup-- they were taking care of it. And I even had a job at the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- from the British army, there was a Red Cross. And I-- I helped them serve food and-- for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: In cigarettes. There were no money then. But with cigarettes, you could buy everything. But we had enough food. And that was the most important. And I gained the weight. And I got back to myself, as you see on the picture. I got even heavy, chubby. And what happened was I wanted to emigrate to Israel. And I went into a kibbutz, which they organized us. And they taught us different things, even the language, start speaking Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Bergen-Belsen also. And we started off with an Aliyah Bet, which was an illegal Aliyah. And British caught us. And they had to turn-- we had turn around and come back to camp. We couldn't make it. Then after a short while, I received a letter from my sister that I should not emigrate to Israel because her husband's family is taking them out to the States. They lived in very difficult conditions. That was in '46. There was a war going on. She had the baby. And they struggled. There was very, very hard times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her baby survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was in Israel she had the baby. And she was reunited with her husband. And she had the baby in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her first baby did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, the first didn't. And she-- I should try to register myself, the American consulate, and try to come to the States. We should be together. So that's what I did. I went to Frankfurt, which was American zone. I registered myself. And I came-- got from the Joint help. I got an affidavit to come to the States, which was in 1947, beginning of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt, I was from the fall, must have been from October till February.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing during that time there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived by a German family. They had to rent their extra rooms to us. And I lived with them. And my husband, who I met in the kibbutz, also, he got an apartment there. And he was very much for going to Israel. But because of me, he didn't want to be separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband on the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kibbutz. He was also there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a social hall. And everybody knew each other. They were introduced to each other. We ate together. And-- and he changed his plans because of me. And I felt bad because he had a brother with him, who also, they were very much pro-Israel. And I didn't want to separate them. The brother didn't want to come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him he should go to the States-- to Israel. And then we're going to meet. I'm going to come to Israel after-- I'm going to follow him. I'm going to meet my sister, see my sister. Then I'll follow you. But he didn't want to OK that. And he didn't listen. And so both of us registered to the-- in the consulate. And I came in '47 before him. And then he followed me a few weeks later. How did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What boat? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was Marine Perch, the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a long journey. It was 12 days. And it was a stormy time of the year. It was a small boat. It was a army boat. And I had a difficult time. I got very sick on the boat. But I made it. And I was very happy to come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on the boat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 80 of us, [NON-ENGLISH] youngsters, friends that I met in Germany. And we made friends on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about coming to the United States? Where did you sail to?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, I had a social worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: To New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they wanted me to leave New York because everybody wanted to stay in New York. And they wanted to spread us out to other cities too. They wanted me to go to Chicago. And I had nobody there. And those who had relatives here and they had some contact with them or went to them, they stayed in New York. I had nobody. And I said, I'm expecting my fiance. And I know he wants to come to New York. He has some relatives here. So they let me stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like the first time you saw New York? What was your first image?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had an unpleasant experience. When we got here, the boat docked. And everybody went off the boat. And a few of us-- they didn't let us. They didn't let us go. They took the papers, our papers, our documents. And they told us to stay in a line till-- whatever, they didn't give us no details. It was dark already. It was night time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the dock?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dock? In-- in Brooklyn, where the docks are, right? Exactly what name of pier, I don't remember. And then a bus took us to Ellis Island, which was a very unpleasant experience. And they didn't tell us why. They put us in the hospital section in Ellis Island. They took away our clothing. They gave us hospital clothing. And the next day, they took our temperature. And there was a doctor who examined us. And they didn't tell us why. And we didn't have any phone calls. And nobody came to visit. Finally, the second or the third day, somebody from the Joint came and explained it to us that our x-rays what we took in Germany was not clear. And they had to make sure that we are healthy. So they had to check it over. But we were worried. I says, here we are in a free land, and here's like a camp again. And we saw people who were there for a long time, the striped uniform, went to eat lunch, men. And they told us stories that I had heard for such a long time. We went to the library. We saw books and inscribed there that don't give up hope. I was here from this date to this date. During the war, these people came illegal. They were not-- they couldn't go back to their own country. And they were not legal immigrants, so they stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a few days, I went to a home in Bronx. Who's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also from the Joint. And those people who had no place to stay until something developed, temporarily, we stayed there. And my husband was not here yet. And I didn't meet his family yet. So I had nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were just engaged. And a social worker suggested I should go to live with a family who would like to give me a home in Jamaica if I'm willing to. I never met the people. And I said yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of family?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- I trusted her because she said she knows them, and they're very nice people. And I would be more than welcome by them. And they want me to stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish family and religious family. I wanted a Sabbath observer because that's how I wanted to continue. That same day, when I was supposed to go to this family, my husband came from Europe. It was a Friday. I went to see him. He didn't get off the boat yet. I was just waving to him. And I went off to Jamaica. He couldn't understand it. Here I was, waiting for you to-- I was anxious to come, and I was anxious to see him. Yet, we can't see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to this family who lived in Jamaica. Their name was Rabbi Bernard Segel. He was affiliated with the [HEBREW] seminary. Very nice people. And they had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to them on Friday before Shabbat. And they introduced themselves and the children. They were very nice. The little girl must have been seven and the boy about five. And they welcomed me. And I had to fight to hold back tears because it was the first family that I came into normal family life after-- since the war. And the little boy was very cute. He came over to me. He says, you know, my father came from Poland. So you know, it made me feel comfortable that he is also from the other side. I lived with them from Pesach. That was maybe March, April, until I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '47, '47. And they very nice. It was very nice. And I kept up the friendship with them all these years. And they came to my wedding. And I went to their-- the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH] And it was a nice experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for you to adjust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard, very hard, very hard. I couldn't talk about my background. And sort of people were afraid to ask, or they were afraid to listen the stories. I don't know. But I wasn't ready to talk. I just couldn't come to terms to accept what had happened. So I just kept it, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We had a lot in common because the background is the same, and he also went through a lot. But then the father, Rabbi Segel, came to say goodnight the little girl, who I slept-- I shared a room with her-- and brought her something, up a drink or kissed her, then I started crying. And nobody saw because it was sad, here I'm alone. And they showed me all their family background, the people. I didn't realize that when they got married and they sailed to England and pictures on the newspaper cut out and stories from their family. And I said, I also have a story, but I can't tell in myself. I'm also somebody. I came from somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when you came from the concentration camp, they didn't take you as, I don't know, a normal human being. Shall I say that? Because they know we were different. But inside, we wanted to be recognized as somebody, that we have a past and we belonged. So it was a hard-- it was an adjustment, a big adjustment. Did you know a lot of other survivors here in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we kept in touch with them. And we always said, we're not going to be the same. We cannot be the same as other people, even if you're going to survive. We are hurt. And we are wounded. And it's going to reflect on our lives. And so it was. It did. We didn't realize it. We didn't realize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got married, I had friends who went through the same. We kept together. There were boys who were single yet. They used to meet in our house. We had a nice social life socializing with them. When I got married, and I had nobody-- my sister wasn't here yet when I got married. Strangers around me by my wedding, somebody who made a wedding as a gift. And everybody came, you know, just to participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I had my child, I didn't want to talk-- when they were little, I didn't want to talk about it. Only when they asked me, how come we don't have grandparents? Then I told them, they're not alive anymore. But I didn't go in details. I didn't want to sadden them. But my children knew. Even if I didn't talk, they know we are different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they felt-- later they told me, they felt guilty that they have a better life than I had, and they have a better-- they have parents and they live-- And they were only friends and associated with children whose parents went through in concentration camp. They had-- they were more comfortable with them. They felt they had the same as background as theirs. And they were closer to those children, which we didn't realize it then. We thought everything is, you know, normal, the way children are supposed to be brought up. And we tried. But we missed our parents, of course. We couldn't share our happiness with them. When my first child was born, I thought it was the greatest gift that I could have gotten, a normal, healthy baby after all I went through because I thought even if I survive, or I'm going to have a long disease, or I'm going to have something, which what I went through is going to leave a mark. But thank god, it didn't. And I had nice children, beautiful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest is Shirley, then my son, Arnold, my daughter, Goldie, and my youngest, Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest, her husband is in public relation. And she's in sales. She is doing very well. The second one is also in sales, my son. My daughter-in-law is a lovely girl. She's home raising children. They have four boys, busy with that. The third one is my daughter Goldie, who is a very ambitious young lady. She has five children. She went back to nursing school now. She wants to be a nurse. My youngest son is also in sales. Runs in the family. He-- they have one little girl. They're happily married. And thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: How important was religion to you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was important because I come from a very strict Orthodox home. And in the camp, also I think helped me because my parents are such strong believers that I thought life is no other way. You have to believe. You have to believe in God. And I still prayed. And I still hoped that I'm going to survive and I should be well and I should find part of my family alive after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any of your other family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found cousins, a few cousins, not many. I found two from my father's side and three from my mother's side. But no aunts or uncles survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to ever visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back seven years ago. I went back for the reason my grandparents are buried there. And I went to the cemetery. I can't go to my parents' because they don't have any. Where should I go? I went to my grandfather, especially. My grandmother, I didn't remember because I was very young when she died. But my grandfather was very close with. And I went to his-- his-- his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sate Mare, Satu Mare. And then I let out all the cries that I couldn't cry before by my parents' grave. And I felt a little better, lighter, after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go back with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town, more or less the same. I didn't go-- I didn't spend much time because we were very limited in time. We rented a driver with a-- with a car and was scarce and they didn't have enough gasoline. It was very bad times then. And I had to be back to take the plane back, so we spent very little time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews left in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few, very few. The synagogue of my grandfather was barricaded with barbed wire. You couldn't go in there. The store didn't existed anymore. The house where we lived was bombed. There was no trace of it anymore, nothing. So it was a very emotional experience. And nothing I found what was ours or belonged to us, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you gone back since?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back this summit again, also to my grandfather's grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like this summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- the circum-- the situation was more free to move around because that was under Russian rules then, seven years ago. And you really had to be careful every step you made because you weren't sure of-- you know, we were-- it was scared. This time is more-- it's freer. And the people are in better-- they live in better conditions. But no Jews around. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have, I think, 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know about what you've gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandchildren-- yes, my grandson, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's oldest, he once interviewed me, which was very emotional, and I couldn't go into details. It was very short. And then last year, my son's second son, the teacher asked him about his background, about his grandparents, where they come from, and what life did they have. And he asked me if I go and tell a story. And in a nutshell, I did, the first time. Yes, that was last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's emotional. It's emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he is-- 15. He was-- he's 16 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I was young. And I looked younger than I was. And they felt sorry for me. And they were kinder to me as the older ones. And I got a little extra here and there, which meant a big difference then. And my sister, my sister. Because of her, I survived. She was very-- she was like a mother to me, very good and very passionate and very caring and very loving.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remain close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close, very close, all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where'd she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She was sick last year. And I visited her more, very often. And I tried to do as much as I could for her. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: She passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: She passed away. She passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what message do you have now for your grandchildren and for everybody who will be studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is that you have to have good intention to others and helpful to others, even if you help a little bit. I saw when you have nothing and somebody was kind to you, it meant a lot. And I always-- when we were in camp and things were bad, but we thought, after this war, never going to be another war, that people will learn to live with each other and no hatred because hate, this [? level ?] hatred wore out. And so many innocent people died because of that, who didn't do no crime in their life. And they were peace-loving and good people and lived peacefully. And then-- then-- this to happen, I thought people will learn from this. And also, not to give up hope. Never, never give up hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'm doubtful today. I'm doubtful. There is still a lot of hatred in the world. And a lot of places, terrible things are still going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very grateful for the people who made this possible, who gave the funds to have this recorded. I always wanted to have this recorded, the memory of my family and the memory of those who-- who died. And it gives me satisfaction that I know it was recorded and they're going to be remembered and they're going to be heard about. It means a lot to me. And I want to thank all those people who are responsible. Mr. Spielberg, I feel very grateful to him, and I think very highly of him. And the other people who I don't even know their name, but I heard that they donated for this fund. And it's-- I think it's a very important thing to have done. And I thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it?
